title: Distributed Lock Service — System Design
description: Design a service that grants mutual exclusion across a fleet: only one client at a time holds a lock on a key, even as clients and nodes fail. The themes are consensus-backed durable lock state, leases with TTL (so a dead holder doesn't block forever), fencing tokens (to defeat the lock's fundamental safety gap), and leader election.

nodes.label: Client (Worker)
nodes.description: Acquires a lock to do exclusive work; renews and releases it.

nodes.label: Lock Service API
nodes.description: Acquire/renew/release; issues fencing tokens; routes to the consensus group.

nodes.label: Consensus Leader
nodes.description: The elected leader of the replica group that serializes lock writes.

nodes.label: Consensus Followers
nodes.description: Replicate the log; one becomes leader on failure (Raft/Paxos).

nodes.label: Replicated Log
nodes.description: The agreed, durable sequence of lock operations (source of truth).

nodes.label: Lock State (Replicated)
nodes.description: Current lock holders + lease expiry, applied from the log.

nodes.label: Lease Sweeper
nodes.description: Expires leases whose holder didn't renew in time.

nodes.label: Protected Resource
nodes.description: The thing being guarded (a file, a DB write, a job) that checks the token.

functional: Acquire an exclusive lock on a key; block/fail-fast if held.
Renew (extend) a held lock; release it explicitly.
Automatically release a lock if the holder dies (no permanent deadlock).
Provide a way to make protected actions safe even if a lock is wrongly believed held.
Serve as a primitive for leader election (one holder = the leader).
nonFunctional: Safety: at most one client holds a given lock at any time (mutual exclusion).
Liveness: a crashed holder's lock is eventually released (via lease expiry).
Durable + highly available: survive node failures without losing lock state or granting two holders.
Correctness under the worst case: process pauses (GC), clock skew, and network partitions.
Low latency for acquire/renew on the common path.

capacity.label: Lease TTL
capacity.note: Short enough to recover fast, long enough to renew.

capacity.label: Consistency
capacity.note: Correctness over availability for lock grants.

api.description: Acquire the lock with a lease TTL; returns a fencing token if granted.
Extend the lease before it expires (heartbeat).

dataModel.name: lock
dataModel.note: Current lock state, applied from the replicated log.

dataModel.name: replicated_log
dataModel.note: Agreed, durable sequence of lock ops (the source of truth).

patterns.name: Consensus-replicated state
patterns.what: Replicate lock operations via Raft/Paxos so a quorum agrees; survive node loss without divergence.
patterns.whenToUse: Lock/config state that must be durable, consistent, and highly available.

patterns.name: Lease with TTL
patterns.what: Grant a lock for a bounded time; the holder renews, or it auto-expires.
patterns.whenToUse: Avoiding permanent deadlock when a holder dies.

patterns.name: Fencing token
patterns.what: Issue a monotonically increasing token with each grant; resources reject stale tokens.
patterns.whenToUse: Defeating the lock's fundamental gap (a paused holder acting after expiry).

patterns.name: Leader election (lock as primitive)
patterns.what: Holding a well-known lock = being the leader; lease loss = stepping down.

patterns.name: Lease sweeping / failure detection
patterns.what: Expire leases whose holder stopped renewing within the TTL + grace.
patterns.whenToUse: Detecting dead holders and reclaiming locks.

patterns.name: Linearizable (CP) operations
patterns.what: Lock grants go through the leader/quorum so there's a single, total order.
patterns.whenToUse: When two holders would be catastrophic (choose CP over availability).

steps.title: 1. Consensus-Backed Lock State
description: A lock service's one job is mutual exclusion, so its state must never diverge — two nodes can't each think a different client holds the lock. Replicate the lock state across a small group (3 or 5 nodes) via a consensus protocol (Raft/Paxos): one leader proposes each lock operation, a quorum agrees and appends it to a replicated log, and that committed log is the source of truth.

This makes operations linearizable (a single total order) and durable (survives a minority of nodes failing) — deliberately choosing CP (consistency over availability): if quorum is lost, the service refuses grants rather than risk two holders.
Lock state (holder, expiry, token per key) is a deterministic application of the committed log, so all replicas agree.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why must lock state go through consensus rather than a single fast database?

concepts.definition: A leader + quorum agree on a replicated log of operations; committed entries are durable and ordered.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees a single, consistent view of who holds a lock even through node failures.
concepts.example: Acquire is committed only after a quorum replicates it.

whyNow: Consensus is the foundation — mutual exclusion demands a single agreed source of truth; everything else (leases, fencing) sits on top.

recap.after: Durable, linearizable lock state via consensus.
recap.newRisk: A client can acquire a lock and then die holding it — locking everyone out forever.

traps.trap: Storing lock state in a single (or async-replicated) database.
traps.why: A failover/partition can produce two nodes that each grant the lock -> two holders.

steps.title: 2. Leases with TTL (No Permanent Deadlock)
description: If a lock were held until explicitly released, a client that crashes while holding it would block the resource forever. Grant locks as leases with a TTL: the holder must periodically renew (heartbeat) to keep it; if it stops (crash, hang, partition), the lease expires and the lock is reclaimed.

A lease sweeper (or the leader) expires leases past their TTL and frees the key. This gives liveness — a dead holder's lock is automatically released — at the cost of choosing a TTL: too short risks expiring a slow-but-alive holder; too long delays recovery.
Renew goes through consensus too (it extends committed state), but is the common, cheap path.
steps.decisionPrompt: A worker acquires a lock and then crashes. How do you avoid locking the resource forever?

concepts.definition: A lock granted for a bounded time, kept alive by heartbeats, auto-expiring otherwise.
concepts.whyItMatters: Provides liveness — a dead holder's lock is reclaimed without manual intervention.
concepts.example: 15s lease; renew every 5s; miss renewals -> expire and free.

whyNow: Leases are the standard fix for the dead-holder deadlock; the TTL tradeoff is the practical decision.

recap.before: Locks held until release (deadlock on crash).
recap.newRisk: A holder can be PAUSED (GC) past expiry, lose the lock, then resume and act — thinking it still holds it.

steps.title: 3. Fencing Tokens (The Fundamental Gap)
description: Leases introduce the classic distributed-lock hazard: a holder pauses (a long GC, a slow disk) past its lease expiry; the service grants the lock to someone else; the paused holder wakes up still believing it holds the lock and acts on the resource — now two clients act. No lock service alone can prevent this.

The fix is fencing tokens: each grant returns a monotonically increasing token. The holder includes its token in every operation on the protected resource, and the resource rejects any token lower than the highest it has seen. The paused holder's stale (lower) token is refused.

This is the key correctness insight: the lock provides mutual exclusion in the common case, but the fencing token is what makes the protected action actually safe under pauses/skew. The resource must check the token.
steps.decisionPrompt: A holder pauses for a GC, its lease expires, another client gets the lock, then the first wakes up and writes. How do you prevent corruption?

concepts.definition: A monotonic token issued per grant; the protected resource rejects any token older than the latest it has accepted.
concepts.whyItMatters: Defeats the paused-holder problem that leases alone can't — the real safety mechanism.
concepts.example: Holder writes with token 42; after expiry the new holder gets 43; the resource refuses a later write with 42.

whyNow: Fencing tokens are THE senior insight of distributed locking — naming the pause/expiry gap and that the resource must check tokens is the differentiator.

recap.after: Fencing tokens make protected actions safe.
recap.newRisk: The service itself must stay available and elect a new leader if one fails.

traps.trap: Trusting the lock alone for safety (no fencing).
traps.why: A paused/partitioned holder can act after losing the lock, corrupting the resource.
traps.instead: Issue fencing tokens; the resource rejects stale ones.

steps.title: 4. Leader Election as a Lock
description: A distributed lock is the primitive for leader election: whoever holds a well-known lock (e.g. 'scheduler-leader') is the active leader; losing the lease means stepping down. This is exactly how systems pick a single active instance.

The same mechanics apply: the leader renews its lease to stay leader; if it crashes/pauses, the lease expires and another instance acquires the lock and takes over. The fencing token becomes the leader's epoch — downstream systems reject actions from a stale (lower-epoch) ex-leader.
So the lock service both is internally leader-elected (its consensus group) and provides leader election to its clients — the same idea at two levels.
steps.decisionPrompt: How would a job scheduler use this lock service to guarantee exactly one active scheduler?

concepts.definition: Holding a designated lock = being leader; lease loss = stepping down; the token = the leader epoch.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single, reusable primitive for single-active-instance coordination.
concepts.example: Scheduler acquires 'scheduler-leader'; ex-leader's lower epoch is fenced out.

whyNow: Showing the lock is the building block for leader election connects it to the broader systems (job scheduler, etc.) and reuses fencing as the epoch.

recap.after: Locks as a leader-election primitive with fenced epochs.
recap.newRisk: The lock service's own leader can fail or partition — it must recover safely.

steps.title: 5. Failure, Partitions, and the Worst Cases
description: The consensus group handles its own leader failure: if the leader dies, the followers elect a new one (a higher term); in-flight uncommitted ops are resolved by the protocol, and committed lock state is preserved — no two holders.

Network partitions are handled by quorum: only the side with a majority can commit grants; the minority side refuses to grant or renew (it can't reach quorum), so it can never independently hand out a lock. This is the CP choice — under partition the lock service sacrifices availability on the minority side to preserve safety.

Clock skew is why you don't trust local clocks for expiry decisions: lease expiry is judged relative to the consensus group's committed timeline (and fencing tokens backstop any timing error). The combination — quorum + fencing — is what makes it correct even in the nasty cases.
steps.decisionPrompt: The network partitions the consensus group 2-vs-3. Which side can grant locks, and why is that safe?

concepts.definition: Only a majority partition can commit; the minority refuses grants, preserving single-holder safety.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents two partitions from independently granting the same lock (split-brain).
concepts.example: 2-node side can't reach quorum -> refuses; 3-node side continues safely.

whyNow: Partition/failure behavior is where lock correctness is truly tested; quorum + fencing + not trusting clocks is the staff-level answer.

recap.after: Safe through leader failure, partitions, and clock skew.
recap.newRisk: Many keys and high acquire/renew volume must scale without a single group bottlenecking.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Followers elect a new leader (new term); committed lock state survives; no lock is granted twice.

failureDrills.scenario: A client is partitioned from the service while holding a lock.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It can't renew -> its lease expires -> the lock is reclaimed; its stale fencing token is rejected if it later acts.

steps.title: 6. Scaling and Operating the Lock Service
description: A single consensus group has limited throughput (every write is a quorum round-trip). Scale by sharding the key space across multiple independent consensus groups, each owning a subset of keys — locks on different keys don't contend, so throughput scales horizontally while each group stays small and fast.

Keep leases short enough for timely recovery but long enough that renewals aren't excessive; clients renew well before expiry to tolerate a missed heartbeat. The API tier is stateless and routes a key to its group's current leader.

Operationally: the replicated log is the durable truth (snapshot + compact it so it doesn't grow forever); lock state is rebuildable from the log. Expose lock-held metrics and detect stuck/long-held locks. The whole design's value is correctness, so favor refusing service (CP) over ever granting two holders.
steps.decisionPrompt: One consensus group can't handle the lock throughput. How do you scale without risking safety?
whyNow: Closing on key-space sharding + log compaction + CP operating stance shows the service scales while never compromising the single-holder guarantee.

recap.after: Key-sharded consensus groups; compacted logs; CP operating posture.
recap.newRisk: Cross-key transactions (locking multiple keys atomically) — bounded by ordering/avoiding or a higher-level protocol.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard keys across independent groups; locks on distinct keys don't contend.
Snapshot + compact; rebuild state from snapshot + tail.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Distributed Lock Service
finalDesign.description: Lock operations go through a consensus group (Raft/Paxos): a leader proposes, a quorum replicates to a durable log, and lock state (holder, expiry, fencing token per key) is the committed log applied deterministically — linearizable and CP, so it never grants two holders. Locks are leases with a TTL that holders renew; a sweeper expires dead holders' leases for liveness. Each grant returns a monotonically increasing fencing token, and protected resources reject stale tokens — defeating the paused-holder gap leases can't. The service is internally leader-elected and offers leader election to clients (lock = leader, token = epoch). Under partition only the majority grants locks; the key space shards across independent groups to scale, with compacted logs and a correctness-first (CP) posture.

functional.how: Consensus-committed lock ops via the API.
Fencing tokens checked by the resource.

say: Confirm the hard guarantee: at most one holder, ever (safety > availability).
Pin liveness (no deadlock on crash) and correctness under pauses/partitions.
Note it's a CP system by design.
Leases with TTL + renewal for liveness.
The paused-holder gap and why fencing is required.
Lock as a leader-election primitive (token = epoch).
Partition/quorum behavior, clock skew, scaling by key sharding.
Tradeoffs: lease TTL length, CP availability sacrifice, sharding for throughput.
Mention multi-key locking and read/shared locks as extensions.

expectations: Uses a lock with a TTL lease.
May store state in one DB and miss fencing/consensus.
Knows fencing tokens defeat the pause gap.
Reasons about the paused-holder problem and clock skew precisely.
Designs key-sharded scaling + log compaction; lock as election primitive.

followUps: Why can't a lease alone guarantee safety, and exactly how does a fencing token fix it?
How do you choose the lease TTL, and what breaks if it's too short or too long?
Under a network partition, which side can grant locks and why?
How would you support locking multiple keys atomically?
Why is this a CP system, and when would a weaker (AP) lock be acceptable?
